items.text: We did not have any dry development or exploratory wells drilled for the years 2021, 2020 or 2019.
items.rationale: A factual historical statement about drilling activity with no forward-looking risk implication.

items.text: Such rules may impact the ultimate timing of our exploration and development plans.
items.rationale: Generic statement that rules may impact timing, lacking specifics.

items.text: In recent years, federal and state policy makers and regulators have increasingly implemented or proposed new laws and regulations designed to reduce methane emissions and other GHG, which have included mandates for new leak detection and retrofitting requirements, stricter emission standards and a proposed fee on methane emission leaks.
items.rationale: Introduces specific new regulatory trend on methane/GHG emissions including leak detection mandates and proposed fees.

items.text: For example, in November 2021, the Pipeline and Hazardous Materials Safety Administration issued a final rule significantly expanding reporting and safety requirements for operators of gas gathering pipelines, including previously unregulated pipelines.
items.rationale: Cites a specific new PHMSA rule expanding reporting/safety requirements for gas gathering pipelines.

items.text: This decline contributed to a swift and material deterioration in commodity prices in early 2020.
items.rationale: Factual historical statement about price deterioration, not a new forward risk.

items.text: Moreover, the COVID-19 pandemic has contributed to disruption and volatility in our supply chain, which has resulted, and may continue to result, in increased costs and delays for pipe and other materials needed for our operations.
items.rationale: Introduces specific supply chain disruption and cost/delay risk tied to COVID-19.

items.text: Our operations also may be adversely affected if we or our service providers are unable to retain sufficient personnel or such personnel are unable to work effectively, including because of 15 illness, quarantines, government actions or other restrictions in connection with the pandemic.
items.rationale: Introduces specific personnel/labor availability risk due to pandemic-related illness or restrictions.

items.text: Moreover , our ability to perform certain functions could be disrupted or otherwise impaired by new business practices arising from the pandemic .
items.rationale: Further elaborates on pandemic-related operational disruption already introduced.

items.text: Our business requires significant capital to find and acquire new reserves.
items.rationale: Generic statement about capital needs, standard industry language.

items.text: Although we plan to primarily fund these activities from cash generated by our operations, we have also from time to time relied on other sources of capital, including by accessing the debt and equity capital markets.
items.rationale: General statement about funding sources, not a new specific risk.

items.text: There can be no assurance that these or other financing sources will be available in the future on acceptable terms, or at all.
items.rationale: Generic cautionary statement about financing availability.

items.text: If we are unable to generate sufficient funds from operations or raise additional capital for any reason, we may be unable to replace our reserves, which would adversely affect our business, financial condition and results of operations.
items.rationale: Expands on the consequences of insufficient capital, tying it to reserve replacement risk.

items.text: Although the pause on leasing was lifted in June 2021, the Department of the Interior subsequently issued its report on the federal leasing program in November 2021.
items.rationale: Provides specific new regulatory development regarding federal leasing pause and report.

items.text: The report recommended various changes to the program, including, among other things, increasing royalty and rental rates, enhancing bonding requirements and applying a more rigorous land-use planning process prior to leasing.
items.rationale: Details specific new regulatory recommendations affecting leasing costs and requirements.

items.text: However, certain of the report’s recommendations require Congressional actions, and we cannot predict to what extent, if any, the Department of the Interior may be able to promulgate rules implementing the recommendations of the November 2021 report.
items.rationale: Continues discussion of same regulatory uncertainty introduced in prior sentences.

items.text: For example, New Mexico implemented protocols in November 2021 requiring operators to take various actions with respect to salt-water disposal wells within a specified proximity of certain seismic activity, including a requirement to limit injection rates if the seismic event is of a certain magnitude.
items.rationale: Introduces specific new state regulation on saltwater disposal wells in New Mexico.

items.text: Separately, the Railroad Commission of Texas recently imposed limits on certain salt-water disposal well activities in portions of the Midland Basin.
items.rationale: Introduces specific new Texas regulatory action limiting disposal well activities.

items.text: These or similar actions directed at our operating areas could limit the takeaway capacity for produced water in the impacted area, which could increase our operating expense, require us to curtail our development plans or otherwise adversely impact our operations.
items.rationale: Describes the operational impact of the newly introduced disposal well regulations.

items.text: In addition, new taxes are from time to time proposed (such as minimum taxes on net book income) and, if enacted, could adversely impact us.
items.rationale: Introduces specific new tax proposal risk (minimum tax on net book income).

items.text: For example, the EPA proposed rules in November 2021 that if adopted would, among other things, (i) broaden methane and volatile organic compounds emission reduction requirements for certain oil and gas facilities, including a zero-emission standard for pneumatic controllers, and (ii) impose standards to eliminate venting of associated gas, and require capture and sale of gas where sale line is available, at new and existing oil wells.
items.rationale: Details specific new EPA proposed rules on methane and VOC emissions.

items.text: The EPA plans to issue a supplemental proposal in 2022 containing additional requirements not included in the November 2021 proposed rule and anticipates the issuance of a final rule by the end of the year.
items.rationale: Continues detail on the same EPA rulemaking process already introduced.

items.text: Congress also recently considered legislation that included a proposal to apply a fee on certain methane emissions from oil and gas facilities, although the fate of this “methane fee” is uncertain at this time.
items.rationale: Introduces specific new legislative proposal for a methane emissions fee.

items.text: President Biden subsequently announced a target of reducing economy-wide net GHG emissions in the U.S. by 50% to 52% below 2005 levels by 2030.
items.rationale: Introduces specific new federal GHG reduction target affecting regulatory landscape.

items.text: At the international level, the United States and the European Union jointly announced the launch of a Global Methane Pledge at the 26th Conference of the Parties in November 2021, pursuant to which over 100 participating countries have pledged to a collective goal of reducing global methane emissions by at least 30% from 2020 levels by 2030.
items.rationale: Introduces specific new international commitment (Global Methane Pledge) affecting regulatory risk.

items.text: The increasing attention to climate change may result in further claims or investigations against us, and heightened societal or political pressures may increase the possibility that liability could be imposed on us in such matters without regard to our causation of, or contribution to, the asserted damage or violation, or to other mitigating factors.
items.rationale: Expands on existing climate-related litigation and liability risk with more detail on causation standards.

items.text: 18 F inally, c limate change may also result in various enhanced physical risks, such as an increased frequency or intensity of extreme weather events or changes in meteorological and hydrological patterns, that may adversely impact our operations.
items.rationale: Introduces new physical risk category from climate change (extreme weather, hydrological changes).

items.text: Such physical risks may result in damage to our facilities or otherwise adversely impact our operations, such as if we are subject to water use curtailments in response to drought, or demand for our products, such as to the extent warmer winters reduce demand for energy for heating purposes.
items.rationale: Further details the physical risk impacts already introduced in the prior sentence.

items.text: These and the other risks discussed above could result in additional costs, new restrictions on our operations and reputational harm to us, as well as reduce the actual and forecasted demand for our products.
items.rationale: Generic summary statement referencing previously discussed risks without new specifics.

items.text: These affects in turn could impair or lower the value of our assets, including by resulting in uneconomic or “stranded” assets, and otherwise adversely impact our profitability, liquidity and financial condition.
items.rationale: Expands on financial impact (stranded assets) of previously introduced climate risk.

items.text: In addition, we conduct certain of our operations through joint ventures in which we may share control with third parties, and the other joint venture participants may have interests or goals that are inconsistent with those of the joint venture or us.
items.rationale: Introduces new specific risk regarding joint venture control and conflicting interests.

items.text: These actions may be prompted or exacerbated by unfavorable recommendations or ratings from proxy advisory firms or other third parties, including with respect to our performance under ESG metrics.
items.rationale: Introduces new specific risk regarding proxy advisory firm ESG ratings influencing shareholder actions.

items.text: Our Ability to Declare and Pay Dividends and Repurchase Shares Is Subject to Certain Considerations Dividends, whether fixed or variable, and share repurchases are authorized and determined by our Board of Directors in its sole discretion and depend upon a number of factors, including the Company’s financial results, cash requirements and future prospects, as well as such other factors deemed relevant by our Board of Directors.
items.rationale: Standard descriptive language about dividend/repurchase discretion, not a new risk.

items.text: We can provide no assurance that we will continue to pay dividends or authorize share repurchases at the current rate or at all.
items.rationale: Generic no-assurance statement about future dividends/repurchases.

items.text: Any elimination of, or downward revision in, our dividend payout or share repurchase program could have an adverse effect on the market price of our common stock.
items.rationale: Expands on the consequence of dividend/repurchase changes already contextualized in prior sentence.
